symptoms.symptom_name: Completely asymptomatic with normal exercise tolerance (plays competitive soccer)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Full exertional capacity without symptoms strongly supports a benign murmur and a structurally normal heart.
Significant aortic stenosis would typically cause exertional symptoms, which are absent.

symptoms.symptom_name: No cyanosis/clubbing, no infant feeding problems or cyanosis in infancy

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of cyanosis and a normal infancy argue against significant congenital structural disease.

symptoms.symptom_name: Normal growth and development; no frequent respiratory infections

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Normal growth without failure to thrive or recurrent infections argues against a significant left-to-right shunt.

symptoms.symptom_name: No family history of sudden cardiac death or congenital/inherited heart disease

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of a concerning family history lowers the prior probability of inherited/structural cardiac disease.

symptoms.symptom_name: Soft short systolic ejection murmur (<=2/6) at the LLSB, decreasing on standing, with normal S2 splitting and normal apex/pulses

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A soft, standing-attenuated systolic murmur with normal S2 splitting and normal pulses is characteristic of an innocent murmur.
Normal (not fixed/widely split) S2 argues against an ASD.

symptoms.symptom_name: Structurally normal heart on echocardiography (if performed)

investigation_details.expected_result: Structurally and functionally normal heart, confirming the innocent nature of the murmur
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: Not required for a classic innocent murmur; reserved for concerning features or specific parental-reassurance decisions with the GP.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A normal echocardiogram confirms the structurally normal heart if imaging is undertaken.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: A completely asymptomatic, thriving, active child with no cyanosis, normal growth and no concerning family history presents the classic no-red-flag picture of an innocent murmur, warranting reassurance and routine confirmation rather than emergency assessment.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Routine in-person clinical auscultation by a GP
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Confirms the benign auscultatory features of an innocent murmur; the appropriate first step, not urgent.
Not routinely required for a classic innocent murmur in an asymptomatic child; reserved for concerning features or specific reassurance decisions with the GP/paediatrician.
Unnecessary over-triage for a well, thriving child with an incidental murmur and no red flags.
